coredata.dc:title: Solid-state red emissive carbon dots based on high boiling point solvents for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jiangchen
Li, Qiang
Liu, Xinghua
Zheng, Jingxia
Chen, Lin
Yang, Yongzhen
Su, Chunhui
coredata.dc:description: A simple and rapid one-step microwave-assisted method was employed to synthesize solid-state red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) using phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride in a high-boiling point solvent. Raw material ratios and solvent types were investigated. The optimal emission of R-CDs at 625 nm was achieved when the molar ratio was 3:1:6 in a 50% N, N-dimethyl formamide/water for 15 min. When formamide and water (v/v = 5:95) were used, R-CDs with longer emission (640 nm) were obtained and the reaction time was shortened to 3 min, enhancing both efficiency and cost-effectiveness. Both R-CDs maintained >96% fluorescence intensity after UV exposure and <5% weight loss at 200°C. Combined with polyvinyl pyrrolidone, R-CDs baesed red LEDs with CIE coordinates (0.67, 0.33) and (0.62, 0.38) were achieved. Combined with blue/green CDs, white LEDs showed color rendering indexes of 91–92. This study advances solid-state red CDs for high-quality white LEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical materials
Photonics
Materials synthesis

Preparation of solid-state red emissive CDs
Optimization of the preparation process for R-CDs-D
Optimization of the preparation process for R-CDs-F

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, XRD, and Raman characterization of the R-CDs
(A) TEM images of R-CDs-D, inset: HRTEM images and particle size distribution of R-CDs-D.
(B) TEM images of R-CDs-F, inset: HRTEM images and particle size distribution of R-CDs-F.
(C) XRD patterns of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F.
(D) Raman spectra of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F.
FT-IR and XPS charaterization of the R-CDs
(A) FT-IR spectra of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F.
(B) XPS full survey of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F.
(C) High resolution C 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs-D.
(D) High resolution N 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs-D.
(E) High resolution O 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs-D.
(F) High resolution F 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs-D.
(G) High resolution C 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs-F.
(H) High resolution N 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs-F.
(I) High resolution O 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs-F.
(J) High resolution F 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs-F.
Optical properties of the R-CDs
(A) Photographs of R-CDs-D powder under sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right).
(B) Photographs of R-CDs-F powder under sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right).
(C) The PL emission spectra of R-CDs-D powder under different excitation wavelengths.
(D) The PL emission spectra of R-CDs-F powder under different excitation wavelengths.
Optical properties of the R-CDs
(A) UV-vis spectra of R-CDs-D and corresponding ethanol solution with different concentrations.
(B) UV-vis spectra of R-CDs-F and corresponding ethanol solution with different concentrations.
Optical properties of the R-CDs
(A–F) PL spectra of R-CDs-D ethanol solutions with different concentrations.
(G–L) PL spectra of R-CDs-F ethanol solutions with different concentrations.
The proposed fluorescent emission mechanism of R-CDs
Optical and thermal stability of the R-CDs
(A) Optical stability of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F.
(B) TGA curves of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F (10°C/min, N2 atmosphere).
Photos of R-CDs and their application in red LEDs
(A) Emission spectrum of R-CDs-D-based red LED, inset: photo of R-CDs-D-based red LED.
(B) CIE chromaticity coordinate of R-CDs-D based red LED.
(C) Emission spectrum of R-CDs-F-based red LED, inset: photo of R-CDs-F-based red LED.
(D) CIE chromaticity coordinate of R-CDs-F based red LED.
Photos of R-CDs and its application in white LEDs
(A) Emission spectrum of white LED based R-CDs-D, inset: photo of white LED based R-CDs-D.
(B) CIE chromaticity coordinate of white LED based R-CDs-D.
(C) Emission spectrum of white LED based R-CDs-F, inset: photo of white LED based R-CDs-F.
(D) CIE chromaticity coordinate of white LED based R-CDs-F.

head.ce:title: Solid-state red emissive carbon dots based on high boiling point solvents for white light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and rapid one-step microwave-assisted method was employed to synthesize solid-state red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) using phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride in a high-boiling point solvent. Raw material ratios and solvent types were investigated. The optimal emission of R-CDs at 625 nm was achieved when the molar ratio was 3:1:6 in a 50% N, N-dimethyl formamide/water for 15 min. When formamide and water (v/v = 5:95) were used, R-CDs with longer emission (640 nm) were obtained and the reaction time was shortened to 3 min, enhancing both efficiency and cost-effectiveness. Both R-CDs maintained >96% fluorescence intensity after UV exposure and <5% weight loss at 200°C. Combined with polyvinyl pyrrolidone, R-CDs baesed red LEDs with CIE coordinates (0.67, 0.33) and (0.62, 0.38) were achieved. Combined with blue/green CDs, white LEDs showed color rendering indexes of 91–92. This study advances solid-state red CDs for high-quality white LEDs.

ce:section.ce:para:
In recent years, with the rapid development of solid-state lighting, light-emitting diodes (LEDs) have been extensively explored owing to their energy efficiency, high brightness, small size, long lifetime, and environmental friendliness. 1
,
2
,
3
,
4 Currently, commercial white LEDs are primarily fabricated by exciting rare-earth-doped yellow YAG (yttrium aluminum garnet) phosphors with blue LEDs. However, the lack of red light components results in a low color rendering index (CRI), typically below 80, significantly limiting the potential of white LEDs for solid-state lighting. 5
,
6
,
7 Red phosphors must be developed to supplement the red light component, thereby enhancing the CRI and practical lighting performance of white LEDs. However, existing phosphor materials for white LEDs, including rare-earth-doped phosphors and semiconductor quantum dots, suffer from complex production processes, high costs, non-renewability, and toxicity issues. 7 Therefore, the development of novel, low-cost, and low-toxicity red phosphor materials is imperative.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of spherical carbon materials with sizes less than 10 nm and abundant surface functional groups, 8 have emerged as a significant member of the carbon nanomaterials family owing to their abundant raw material sources, low cost, excellent photostability, and tunable fluorescence emission wavelengths. 9
,
10 CDs exhibit substantial potential in the field of solid-state lighting phosphors. In recent years, with the continuous advancement in CD research, their fluorescence emission has covered a broad range from the ultraviolet to the entire visible spectrum and even into the near-infrared region. However, so far, nearly all the reported CDs have only demonstrated efficient fluorescence in solution. When in highly concentrated solutions or the solid state, CDs usually exhibit severe fluorescence self-quenching owing to the resonance energy transfer and direct π-π interactions between CDs, a phenomenon known as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect, 11
,
12
,
13
,
14
,
15
,
16 which limits the application of CDs in solid-state lighting and other luminescent fields. The methods for obtaining solid-state CDs can be broadly categorized into two approaches. One is a matrix-assisted method, in which CDs are dispersed directly in solid matrices such as polymer or inorganic salts to suppress ACQ effect. 17 The other approach is the one-step synthesis of self-quenching-resistant solid-state CDs. 18 However, this approach suffers from issues such as poor reproducibility and stability due to the uneven distribution of CDs. Moreover, most solid-state CDs exhibit fluorescence emission primarily in the short-wavelength regions, such as blue and green. 19
,
20
,
21
,
22
,
23 Additionally, solid-state red emissive CDs still face challenges such as complex preparation processes, 24
,
25 and the fluorescence emission mechanisms have yet to be fully elucidated. These factors significantly hinder their application in solid-state lighting. 26 Therefore, developing a convenient and rapid method for preparing solid-state red-emitting CDs is crucial for broadening the luminescence range of solid-state CDs and expanding their applications in solid-state lighting.

To achieve solid-state red emissive CDs, key aspects to consider include the reaction precursors, reaction solvents, and heteroatom doping. First, the hydroxyl groups in phloroglucinol facilitate dehydration and carbonization, leading to the formation of CDs with a large sp2-conjugated structure. The inherent conjugated structure of the benzene ring further enhances the conjugation domain of CDs, promoting a redshift in luminescence. Second, the solvent environment significantly influences the dehydration and carbonization processes of the precursors, thereby determining the structure and fluorescence of CDs. 27 Solvents with different boiling points exhibit varying capabilities for dehydrating and carbonizing precursors. Water, the most commonly used reaction solvent, offers high safety and simplicity for synthesizing CDs. However, its low boiling point limits its effectiveness in dehydrating and carbonizing precursors. 27
,
28

In contrast, high-boiling solvents such as N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), and formamide exhibit a broad solubility range for various precursors and facilitate the dehydration and carbonization processes. Consequently, CDs with an enlarged sp2-conjugated domain are achieved, inducing a redshift in their photo-luminescence spectra. 29 Finally, atoms with high electronegativities, such as N and F, possess strong electron trapping capabilities. The doping of these atoms can effectively reduce the energy gap of CDs, facilitating long-wavelength emission. 30

In this work, a simple and rapid one-step microwave-assisted method is developed to synthesize solid-state red emissive CDs with excellent optical and thermal stability. Phloroglucinol is selected as the carbon source, while urea and ammonium fluoride are utilized as the N and F dopants, respectively. A high-boiling solvent mixture containing DMF or formamide with water serves as the reaction medium. The results reveal that both, the use of high-boiling solvents and F doping, contribute to promoting the redshift of CDs luminescence. Notably, F doping introduces new defect states on the surface of CDs, enhancing solid-state luminescence, which provides a theoretical basis for the synthesis of solid-state red emissive CDs. Finally, solid-state red emissive CDs were employed as phosphors to fabricate white LED devices, achieving a CRI of up to 92. This work provides an experimental foundation for broadening the applications of solid-state red emissive CDs in the field of solid-state lighting.

To obtain solid-state red emissive CDs with superior optical properties, reaction parameters were optimized based on the principle of the single-factor variable method. The optimal emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity were taken as the evaluation parameters. The effects of reactant molar ratios, reaction time, and the volume percentage of organic solvents on the fluorescent properties were individually investigated. The solid-state red emissive CDs prepared using DMF as the solvent were denoted as R-CDs-D, while those prepared using formamide as the solvent were denoted as R-CDs-F.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and surface composition of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate the morphology and carbon core structure of the synthesized CDs, transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images, X-ray diffractometry (XRD) patterns, and Raman spectra were measured. TEM images (Figures 1A and 1B) demonstrate the quasi-spherical morphology and uniform dispersion of both R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F. Specifically, the average particle sizes of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F are 2.25 ± 0.25 nm and 3.25 ± 0.25 nm, respectively. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis shows distinct lattice fringes for both CDs, indicating a graphite-like carbon core structure. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm for R-CDs-D corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, while the 0.33 nm spacing for R-CDs-F aligns with the (002) plane, further confirming their graphitic nature. XRD patterns (Figure 1C) exhibit a prominent diffraction peak at approximately 23° for both R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F, which is attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. This further confirms the existence of a graphitic structure within carbon cores. Raman spectra (Figure 1D) illustrate characteristic peaks near 1,360 cm−1 (D-band) and 1620 cm−1 (G-band) for both R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F, corresponding to carbon lattice defects and in-plane stretching vibrations of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, respectively. 31
,
32
,
33 The intensity ratio of the G-band to the D-band (I G /I D) serves as an indicator of graphitization level, with a higher ratio indicating a more graphitized structure. The I G /I D values of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F are 1.6 and 1.8, respectively, which indicates both have a high degree of graphitization. It is noteworthy that the degree of graphitization of R-CDs-F is higher than that of R-CDs-D, demonstrating that the replacement of high-boiling point solvent indeed increases the degree of graphitization of CDs, which is beneficial for red-shifted fluorescence emission of CDs.

To further confirm the surface elemental composition of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization were performed. The absorption peak at 3,330 cm−1 of CDs’ FT-IR spectra is attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H bonds, and the peaks at 1,610, 1,396, 1,330, and 1,230 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C=O, as well as asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–N, C–F, and C–O, respectively (Figure 2 A). These observations suggest the formation of N, F, and O-conjugated aromatic rings within CDs, 34 confirming the successful doping of N and F atoms into both R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F.

The XPS spectra (Figure 2B) reveal that both R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F are composed of C, N, O, and F elements. Notably, as the boiling point of the reaction solvent increases, the C content in the CDs increases while the O content decreases, indicating that high boiling point solvents facilitate the carbonization process of CDs. The surface structure of CDs is further analyzed from high-resolution XPS spectra. In both R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F, the C element exists in the forms of C–C/C=C, C–N, C–O, C=N, and C–F. The O element is present as C=O and C–O, while the F element is solely present as C–F. Additionally, the N element in R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F exists in the forms of pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, and graphitic nitrogen. The presence of N and F elements in the XPS spectra confirms the doping of both N and F atoms into R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F.

When the molar ratio of phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride was 3:1:6, R-CDs-D was prepared in a mixed solvent of DMF and water (with DMF comprising 50% of the total volume) for 15 min, while R-CDs-F was synthesized in a mixed solvent of formamide and water (with formamide constituting 5% of the total volume) for 3 min. The synthesized CDs exhibit impressive solid-state red fluorescence emission properties (Figures 3A and 3B). The optimal emission wavelengths of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F are 625 and 640 nm, and the corresponding solid-state quantum yields (QY) are 3.2% and 3.8%, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra indicate that both CDs possess excitation-independent properties, that is, the emission peak positions remain unchanged despite variations in the excitation wavelength, which means they have similar surface structure, and this is beneficial to improving reproducibility. Additionally, as the excitation wavelength increases from 365 to 465 nm, the fluorescence intensity of both CDs initially decreases and then remains almost unchanged.

The mechanism of the solid-state red emissive CDs was further investigated. Both R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F were firstly dissolved in anhydrous ethanol to prepare different concentration solutions, and then the UV-vis spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were measured. The UV-vis spectra of CDs (Figure 4 ) demonstrate the absorption spectra of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F span the entire ultraviolet and visible light regions, with distinct absorption peaks at 350 and 600 nm. It also reveals that as the concentration increases from 0.0010 to 0.0500 mg/mL, the maximum absorption peak near 200 nm undergoes a redshift, and the absorption peaks near 300 and 465 nm increase in intensity. This suggests that CDs gradually accumulate and agglomerate in ethanol solutions as the concentration increases, and forms aggregates finally.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (Figure 5 ) show that as the solution concentration increases from 0.0010 to 0.0500 mg/mL, the maximum emission peaks of both R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F undergo a red shift. According to literature reports, this red shift in emission wavelength and quenching of emission centers at high concentrations are attributed to reabsorption by CDs aggregates or fluorescence resonance energy transfer phenomenon. 35 Furthermore, multiple fluorescence emission centers are present and exhibit excitation-dependent characteristics at low concentrations. However, at high concentrations, the optimal emission peak gradually becomes independent of the excitation wavelength, which is attributed to the number of emission centers decreasing. Both R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F have similar solid-state emission.

It can be found that R-CDs-F obtained using formamide and water as the solvent, which substitutes DMF and water as a solvent with the same reaction precursors, show a red shift of emission wavelength and high luminescence intensity. It may be attributed to the high boiling point of formamide, which produces a higher actual reaction temperature during the synthesis. This further facilitates a higher carbonization of the carbon source, thereby increasing the crystallization degree of CDs thus promoting a red shift in the fluorescence emission of CDs. Furthermore, the regulation of ammonium fluoride in the reaction demonstrates that its dosage significantly impacts the fluorescence properties of the CDs. Compared with CDs prepared without ammonium fluoride, the optimal emission wavelength of R-CDs-D undergoes a redshift. Morphological and structural characterizations of the CDs reveal a high degree of graphitization (with I G /I D ratios of 1.6 and 1.8, respectively) and the presence of abundant functional groups such as C–F bonds on their surfaces. These carbon core structures and surface features contribute to the red shift in the fluorescence emission of the CDs.

On the other hand, when urea aqueous solution is heated, it undergoes hydrolysis and decomposition to produce volatile gases NH3 and CO2. The release of these gases leaves pores in the solid product, forming a bubble-like structure. This unique structure also prevents direct contact between CDs, further inhibiting the ACQ effect and facilitating the solid-state red light emission of CDs. 37 As a result, R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F exhibit distinct solid-state fluorescence emission behavior.

Appropriate photothermal stability is crucial for phosphors in LEDs. Thus, the optical and thermal stability of CDs were tested and analyzed. Figure 7 A shows the variation of fluorescence intensity for R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F under 365 nm ultraviolet light with different irradiation times. The results indicate that after 60 min of continuous irradiation, the fluorescence intensities of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F remain at approximately 96% and 98% compared with their initial intensity, respectively, demonstrating their excellent photostability. Figure 7B presents the TGA curves of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F under the N2 atmosphere. It can be observed that the weight loss rates of R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F are only 3% and 5% at 200 °C, respectively, which satisfies the application requirement for LED phosphors.

R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F with excellent solid-state fluorescence properties and photothermal stability provide a foundation for photoluminescent phosphors used in LEDs. Thus, LED devices based on two CDs were explored. The emission spectra and Commission Internationale de lEclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagrams are shown in Figure 8 . Both CDs-based red LEDs exhibit a single emission peak within the range of 550–750 nm, originating from R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F, respectively. Additionally, the CIE chromaticity coordinates of the R-CDs-D-based red LED are (0.67, 0.33), while those of the R-CDs-F-based red LED are (0.62, 0.38).

In addition, R-CDs-D and R-CDs-F were mixed with previously prepared blue and green emissive CD phosphors from our group, 38
,
39
,
40 which were further combined with UV chips to fabricate white LEDs. The emission spectra and CIE chromaticity diagrams of R-CDs-based white LEDs are shown in Figures 9A and 9B. The emission peak around 410 nm originates from the blue emissive CDs, while the emission in the range of 550–600 nm is attributed to the green emissive CDs and R-CDs-D phosphor. Taking CRI as the primary parameter, the optimal ratio of the three phosphors was 2:5:3. The corresponding LED has CIE chromaticity coordinate of (0.32, 0.33), correlated color temperature (CCT) of 6239 K, and CRI of 91. The emission spectra and CIE chromaticity diagram of R-CDs-F-based white LEDs are shown in Figures 9C and 9D. Similarly, the emission peak around 410 nm originated from the blue emissive CDs, and the emissions at 550 and 600 nm are due to the green emissive CDs and R-CDs-F phosphor. By adjusting the ratio of the three phosphors with CRI as the primary consideration, the optimal ratio was also found to be 2:5:3. The corresponding LED has CIE chromaticity coordinates of (0.31, 0.31), CCT of 6,874 K, and CRI of 92, which is close to standard white light. This work provides insights into the construction of high-CRI white LEDs.

In this work, solid-state red emissive CDs were synthesized using phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride as reaction precursors, with a mixed solution of DMF/formamide and water served as the reaction solvent, via a one-step microwave-assisted method. Their luminescence mechanism was also explored. The results demonstrate that the prepared R-CDs exhibit excellent optical and thermal properties, retaining over 96% of their initial fluorescence intensity after 60 min of UV light irradiation and exhibiting a weight loss of less than 5% at 200 °C. Furthermore, as the boiling point of the reaction solvent increased, the optimal reaction time shortened from 15 to 3 min, and the maximum emission peak of the CDs shifted from 625 to 640 nm. This red shift in luminescence is attributed to the synergistic effect of high-boiling point solvents and fluorine doping, which facilitates the formation of new defect states on the surface of CDs, thereby enhancing solid-state luminescence. Finally, the solid-state red emissive CDs were mixed with blue- and green emissive CDs, dispersed in polyvinyl pyrrolidone to form a fluorescent film, and combined with UV chips to fabricate white LED devices. These devices achieved a CRI of up to 92, providing both theoretical foundations and practical guidance for the application of CDs-based fluorescent materials in solid-state lighting.

Solid-state R-CDs synthesized using a high-boiling point solvent have a low quantum yield. In the follow-up research, it is expected to further doping other elements to improve the solid-state quantum yield.
Further information and requests for resources and reagents should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Jiangchen Wu (wujiangchen0219@126.com).
All unique/stable reagents generated in this study are available from the lead contact with a completed materials transfer agreement.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data: All data are available in the paper and in supplemental information and/or from the corresponding authors upon reasonable request.
Code: This paper does not report original code.
Additional information: Any additional information required to reanalyze the data reported in this paper is available from the lead contact upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Foundational Research Project of Shanxi Province (20210302123164), Shanxi-Zheda Institute of Advanced Materials and Chemical Engineering (2021SX-TD012), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51972221 and 82172048), Shanxi Center of Technology Innovation for Controlled and Sustained Release of Nanodrugs (202104010911026), The Science and Education Cultivation Fund of the National Cancer and Regional Medical Center of Shanxi Provincial Cancer Hospital (TD2023003), Four “Batches” Innovation Project of Invigorating Medical through Science and Technology of Shanxi Province (2023XM012), Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2024-058).

J.W., validation, writing—original draft; Q.L., data curation; X.L., formal analysis and software; J.Z., formal analysis, investigation, and methodology; L.C., formal analysis, investigation, and methodology; Y.Y., writing—review & editing and project administration; C.S., supervision; all authors reviewed the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing interests.

row.entry: Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute
Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd
Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd

ce:section.ce:para: This work did not need any unique experimental model.

To characterize the morphology and structure of the CDs, various advanced analytical techniques were employed. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2010) was used to visualize the nanoscale morphology of the CDs. X-ray diffractometry (XRD, Rigaku-D/MAX 2500) provided information on the crystalline structure and phase composition. Laser Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw inVia) was utilized to analyze the vibrational modes of the CDs, while X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA DLD) revealed the elemental composition and chemical bonding states at the surface. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, TENSOR27) was applied to identify functional groups present in the CDs. To investigate the optical properties, the absorption spectra, and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CDs were measured using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (U-3900) and a fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4), respectively. These measurements allow for the determination of the optical bandgap and emission characteristics of the CDs. The thermal stability of the CDs was assessed using a thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA, DTG-60), which provided insights into the temperature-dependent decomposition behavior of the CDs. Finally, to evaluate the performance of the white LED devices fabricated using the solid-state red emissive CDs as phosphors, a spectra scan colorimeter (PR-655) was employed. This instrument measured the emission spectra, CIE color coordinates, CCT, and CRI of the LED devices, enabling a comprehensive assessment of their lighting quality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of solid-state red emissive CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride as the reaction precursors, and a mixed solution of water and DMF or formamide as the reaction solvent, solid-state red emissive CDs were prepared via a one-step microwave method. During the preparation, the effects of the molar ratio of the reaction pre-cursors, the volume ratio of the high boiling point solvent, and the reaction time on the luminescent properties of the CDs were investigated. The preparation process is as follows: First, 10 mL of a mixed solution of deionized water and DMF or formamide was measured into a 100 mL beaker. Subsequently, appropriate amounts of phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride were added to the solution. After they were well dispersed in the solvent, the solution was sonicated for 10 min to achieve uniform dispersion. The beaker containing the reaction solution was then placed in a microwave oven and subjected to a reaction at 800 W to obtain the solid-state CDs. The obtained CDs were further ground into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle and stored at low temperatures under a sealed condition for subsequent characterization and performance testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the preparation process for R-CDs-D
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of R-CDs-D preparation was conducted by individually investigating the effect of reactant molar ratio, reaction time, and volume percentage of organic solvent on the fluorescence properties of R-CDs-D, aiming to obtain the optimal preparation parameters.

With the reaction time fixed at 10 min and the volume ratio of DMF in the reaction solvent set to 50%, the effect of the dosage of phloroglucinol on the fluorescence properties of R-CDs-D was investigated. The molar ratio of phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride are varied as 1:1:4, 2:1:4, 3:1:4, and 4:1:4. Figure S1 presents the fluorescence images and corresponding PL spectra of R-CDs-D prepared with varying amounts of phloroglucinol. As the molar ratio of reactants increases from 1:1:4 to 3:1:4, the emission wavelength of R-CDs-D powder red-shifts from 590 to 625 nm. However, when the amount of phloroglucinol is further increased to a reaction ratio of 4:1:4, the reaction product becomes liquid and emits yellow fluorescence under UV light with an emission wavelength of around 550 nm (Figure S1B). This is likely due to the fact that excessive phloroglucinol not only fails to fully participate in the reaction but also disrupts its carbonization, which leads to an incomplete reaction and presents a liquid product. Consequently, R-CDs-D obtained at the molar ratio of 3:1:4 was chosen for further optimization.

When the reaction time was fixed at 10 min and the DMF volume ratio in the reaction solvent was set to 50%, the dosage of urea on the fluorescence properties of R-CDs-D was examined by varying their molar ratio at 3:0:4, 3:0.5:4, 3:1:4, 3:1.5:4, and 3:2:4. Figure S2 shows the photographs of R-CDs-D obtained at different urea concentrations under visible light and UV illumination, along with their PL spectra. It can be observed that the urea dosage significantly influences both the emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity of R-CDs-D. In the absence of urea (3:0:4), its optimal emission peak is located around 610 nm with low fluorescence intensity. As the urea amount increases, the optimal emission peak red-shifts to 625 nm, accompanied by an increase in fluorescence intensity. However, when the urea dosage is further increased, a blue shift of the optical emission peak to 600 nm and a decrease in fluorescence intensity is produced. Therefore, the urea dosage was fixed at a molar ratio of 3:1:4 for subsequent optimization.

When the amounts of phloroglucinol and urea are fixed, the dosage of ammonium fluoride was investigated by varying their molar ratio at 3:1:0, 3:1:2, 3:1:4, 3:1:6, and 3:1:8. Figure S3 presents the photographs of R-CDs-D under visible light and UV illumination, as well as their corresponding PL spectra at different molar ration. It is evident that the emission wavelength of R-CDs-D varies with the increase in ammonium fluoride dosage, while the fluorescence intensity initially increases and then decreases. When the molar ratio is 3:1:6, the highest fluorescence intensity is obtained. This phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that when F elements are doped into R-CDs-D, the N-containing groups on its surface form hydrogen bonds with F atoms, maintaining a certain distance among R-CDs-D, thus preventing the radiative energy transfer process, and producing solid-state luminescence. However, as the F content increases beyond a certain level, the additional F atoms tend to bond with the N-containing groups within R-CDs-D itself, reducing the number of hydrogen bonds among R-CDs-D and shortening their distance, leading to aggregation-induced quenching and a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the optimal molar ratio of 3:1:6 was selected to further optimize the solvent type, solvent volume percentage, and reaction time.

When the molar ratio of the reactant precursor was fixed at 3:1:6, the total volume of the mixed solution was fixed at 10 mL, and the DMF content in the mixed solvent was adjusted to investigate the fluorescence properties of R-CDs-D by 0%, 50%, and 100%, as shown in Figure S4. Without the addition of DMF, the optimal emission peak of CDs is close to 600 nm. As DMF is introduced, the emission wavelength of the CDs red-shifts to around 630 nm, accompanied by a decrease in fluorescence intensity. When the DMF volume content is 50% and 100%, the emission wavelengths are identical, but the fluorescence intensity is higher than the former. This can be explained by the fact that as the DMF content increases, its high polarity and significantly higher boiling point (compared with water) allow the reaction temperature to rise to approximately 150 °C, promoting the carbonization of phloroglucinol. However, excessive DMF leads to a higher degree of carbonization of CDs, which reduces the number of surface functional groups and molecules, thereby reducing their fluorescence properties3. Therefore, a DMF volume percentage of 50% is preferred.

When the DMF volume ratio in the reaction solvent was fixed at 50%, and the molar ratio of reactants was fixed at 3:1:6, the reaction time was varied (10, 15, and 20 min) to investigate its effect on the fluorescence properties of R-CDs-D. The fluorescence performance indicates that when the reaction time is 10 min, the optimal emission peak is around 620 nm with a low fluorescence intensity, as shown in Figure S5. As the reaction time increases, the optimal emission peak red-shifts to approximately 625 nm, accompanied by an increase in fluorescence intensity. However, when the reaction time was further increased, a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity was produced. Therefore, a reaction time of 15 min is preferred.

In summary, the optimal synthesis conditions for R-CDs-D are as follows: a molar ratio of 3:1:6 for phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride, a DMF volume percentage of 50% in the solvent, and a reaction time of 15 min. R-CDs-D obtained under these conditions have optimal fluorescence properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the preparation process for R-CDs-F
ce:section.ce:para: When the molar ratio of phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride was fixed at 3:1:6, the effects of reaction time and volume percentage of organic solvent on the fluorescence properties of R-CDs-F were investigated separately to obtain the optimal preparation parameters for R-CDs-F.

First, the influence of the volume percentage of formamide (1%, 5%, and 10%) in the mixed solvent on the structure and fluorescence properties of the reaction product R-CDs-F was examined. Figures S6A and S6B show photo-graphs of the CDs prepared under different formamide volume percentages under visible light and UV illumination, along with their corresponding PL spectra. It is obvious to see that when the formamide volume percentage is 10%, CDs exhibit almost no fluorescence under UV light. This is attributed to the excessively high content of formamide in the solvent, leading to excessively high reaction temperatures that result in over-carbonization of the reaction products, thereby decreasing their fluorescence properties. As the formamide content decreases in the solution, CDs gradually emit red fluorescence, as shown in Figure S6A. Specifically, when the formamide volume percentage is reduced to 5%, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs gradually increases. However, further decreasing the formamide content to 1% enhances the fluorescence intensity. Moreover, a blue shift in the optimal emission peak position was produced. Therefore, the volume percentage of formamide in the solvent is fixed at 5%.

With the volume percentage of formamide in the mixed solvent fixed at 5%, the influence of various reaction times (2, 3, 4, and 5 min) on the structure and fluorescence properties of the reaction product R-CDs-F was evaluated. Photographs of CDs under daylight and UV illumination, along with their corresponding PL spectra, are presented in Figure S7. As can be observed, when the reaction time is 2 min, CDs exhibit a peak emission around 600 nm with high fluorescence intensity. As the reaction time increases from 2 to 4 min, the peak emission wavelength of the CDs undergoes a redshift to approximately 640 nm. However, upon further increasing the reaction time to 5 min, the emission wavelength experiences a blue shift, accompanied by a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity. The measured reaction temperature is approximately 170 °C. Based on these results, 3 min is considered as the best reaction time.

In summary, the optimal reaction conditions for the preparation of R-CDs-F are as follows: a molar ratio of phloroglucinol, urea, and ammonium fluoride of 3:1:6, a formamide percentage in the solvent of 5%, and a reaction time of 3 min. Compared with using a mixed solution of DMF and water as the solvent, substituting DMF with formamide significantly reduces the volume percentage of organic solvent and the total solvent volume required. Furthermore, the reaction time is shortened from the initial 15 to 3 min. This reduction in both reaction time and organic solvent addition is advantageous for precise control over the degree of carbonization of the carbon source, which is crucial for enhancing the optimal emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity of R-CDs-F.

Fabrication of red LED: To fabricate the red LED, the CD powder was first dispersed in a mixed solution of ethanol and polyvinyl pyrrolidone. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min to ensure uniform dispersion, and then carefully dripped onto the inner wall of an optical lens. The lens, with the CDs solution adhered, was placed in an oven and dried at 55°C to form a fluorescent film. Finally, the optical lens coated with the CDs-based fluorescent film was fixed onto a UV LED chip, thereby obtaining the red LED. This device emits red light upon excitation by the UV LED chip, with the CDs acting as the color-converting phosphor.

Fabrication of white LED: CD powder was combined with previously prepared blue and green emissive CDs from our group in a specific ratio (3:2:5). This mixture was then dispersed in a suitable solvent and sonicated for 10 min to ensure a homogeneous distribution of CDs. After sonication, the solution was dripped onto the inner wall of an optical lens. The lens, with the mixed CDs solution adhered, was subsequently placed in an oven and dried at 55 °C to form a multi-color fluorescent film. This film, upon excitation by UV light, emits a combination of red, green, and blue light, resulting in the emission of white light. Finally, the optical lens coated with the multi-color CDs-based fluorescent film was fixed onto a UV LED chip, and the white LED was obtained.
Figures represent averaged or representative results of multiple independent experiments. Analyses and plots were performed with Origin.
There are no additional resources needed to be declared in this manuscript, additional requests for this can be made by contacting the lead contact.
Supplemental information can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isci.2025.112250.

One-step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescence carbon quantum dots with high product yield and quantum yield. Nanotechnology. 30, 085406. https://doi.org/10.1088/1361-6528/aaf3fb.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient synthesis and photoelectric properties of green carbon quantum dots with high fluorescent quantum yield

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng, J.X., Xie, Y.T., Wei, Y.Y., Yang, Y.Z., Liu, X.G., Chen, Y.K., Xu, B.S. (2020). An efficient synthesis and photoelectric properties of green carbon quantum dots with high fluorescent quantum yield. Nanomaterials. 10, 82. https://doi.org/10.3390/nano10010082.
